beats.text: Here's projection matrix P — entries one-half, one-half, one-half, one-half. It projects everything onto the line through one, one.

Find the eigenvalues. Subtract lambda down the diagonal, take the determinant: one-half-minus-lambda, squared, minus one-fourth equals zero. That factors to lambda times lambda-minus-one equals zero. Eigenvalues: lambda equals one and lambda equals zero.

First eigenvector: lambda equals one. P minus I times x equals zero. Every row becomes negative one-half, one-half — so x-one equals x-two. The eigenvector is one, one. An arrow appears along the projection line itself.
Second eigenvector: lambda equals zero. P times x equals zero. The eigenvector is one, negative-one — perpendicular to the line. An arrow appears pointing in that direction.
Boom — projection matrices always have eigenvalues one and zero. Vectors on the line are unchanged. Vectors perpendicular to it get crushed to zero.
